rationale: Provides an operational assurance-case and guardrail framework for LLM robustness and compliance including code tasks, but not specifically organizational oversight of AI-generated code pipelines.
title: Developing assurance cases for adversarial robustness and regulatory compliance in LLMs

rationale: Offers a governance-first runtime containment and provenance framework for autonomous AI agents that is transferable to coding agents, though the abstract is thin and not code-specific.
title: TRACE: a governance-first execution framework providing architectural assurance for autonomous AI operations

rationale: Describes computable governance optimization for proxy AI using NIST-style monitoring and policy concepts, but the abstract is thin and not tied to software engineering.
title: Computable design space exploration via governance frameworks: joint optimisation of permission boundaries, monitoring thresholds and user transparency policies (for proxy AI)

rationale: Presents a concrete benchmarking framework that includes AI-generated code evaluation with standardized metrics, but in a digital forensics tool-validation setting rather than human oversight at scale.
title: AutoDFBench 1.0: a benchmarking framework for digital forensic tool testing and generated code evaluation

rationale: Focuses on improving repo-level AI code generation capability and architectural fidelity without a governance or human-oversight mechanism.
title: Contract-coding: towards repo-level generation via structured symbolic paradigm

rationale: Directly studies dynamic autonomy and local oversight for coding agents using developer evidence and an operational mechanism for adjusting oversight over time.
title: Hedwig: dynamic autonomy for coding agents under local oversight

rationale: Addresses human participation and oversight in AI-mediated systems, but the abstract is very thin and appears broad rather than software- or code-specific.
title: The geometry of human participation: the structural conditions of legitimacy in AI-mediated systems

rationale: Uses an SLR and practitioner questionnaire to examine GenAI in SDLC cybersecurity, including reliability and integration risks, but oversight of AI-generated code is secondary.
title: Exploring the role of generative AI in enhancing cybersecurity in software development life cycle

rationale: Provides a clause-level AI governance interoperability framework involving human oversight evidence reuse, but it is general compliance infrastructure rather than code-specific oversight.
title: Passporting compliance for AI: operationalising governance via a clause-level crosswalk across EU, ISO/IEC, NIST, and CoE frameworks

rationale: Targets scalable automated code review using multi-agent review-comment generation with human evaluation, making it relevant to oversight capacity under code-review volume.
title: Issue-oriented agent-based framework for automated review comment generation

rationale: Addresses human-in-the-loop decision performance and interpretability effects, but only through broad decision-making theory with a thin abstract and no software context.
title: Why providing humans with interpretable algorithms may, counterintuitively, lead to lower decision-making performance

rationale: Discusses agentic attack governance, identity, and behavioral monitoring, which is transferable to autonomous coding-agent risk but not focused on code oversight.
title: The infrastructure gap: why platform security cannot protect against agentic attacks

rationale: SLR evidence on GenAI risks, professional oversight, and adaptive governance in agile enterprise architecture is strongly transferable but broader than AI-generated code review.
title: Impact and implications of generative AI for enterprise architects in agile environments: a systematic literature review

rationale: Empirically surveys developers on security review, overreliance, and vulnerability concerns for AI-generated code, directly informing organizational oversight needs.
title: My code is less secure with gen AI: surveying developers' perceptions of the impact of code generation tools on security

rationale: Proposes machine-readable governance dimensions for high-risk AI deployment authorization, but it is general AI certification rather than software-pipeline oversight.
title: AI deployment authorisation: a global standard for machine-readable governance of high-risk artificial intelligence

rationale: Presents multi-agent adversarial verification for regulated AI decisions and human-oversight compliance, transferable as an oversight pattern but outside code generation.
title: Verified adversarial reasoning intelligence (VARI): multi-agent adversarial verification for regulated AI decision-making

rationale: Analyzes legal concepts of human oversight in the EU AI Act context, but it is older, legal-theoretical, and not operationalized for software engineering.
title: From 'human control' in international law to 'human oversight' in the new EU act on artificial intelligence

rationale: Proposes a workflow-integrated LLM assistant to detect AI-introduced clones and license risks with developer feedback, giving an operational oversight mechanism for generated code.
title: Towards just-In-time, inclusive clone refactoring

rationale: Empirically studies how static-analysis signal selection improves LLM program repair efficiency, transferable to automated quality gates but not focused on human oversight.
title: An empirical analysis of ESLint information components and their interactions in LLM-based program repair

rationale: Provides strong empirical evidence on overreliance and monitoring reduction in human-automation redundancy, directly transferable to AI-code oversight behavior.
title: Designing human-automation redundancy: overreliance, automation shirking, and the operator’s dilemma

rationale: Presents and evaluates an auditable multi-agent LLM static code review artifact aligned with regulatory reporting, directly relevant to scalable code oversight.
title: Code prism: a multi-agent, multi-LLM, semantic indexing artifact for regulatory code audits — a design science research study

rationale: Surveys LLM-based vulnerability detection in static analysis with practical selection guidance, but focuses on detection capability rather than governance or human oversight.
title: Large language models for vulnerability detection in static code analysis: a survey

rationale: Offers an enterprise AI governance taxonomy around visibility, alignment, and authorization that may structure oversight claims but is not software-code specific.
title: A taxonomy of AI governance approaches: distinguishing visibility, alignment, and authorization

rationale: Directly discusses vibe coding quality limits and proposes formal specification and verification as an oversight mechanism, but appears position-oriented rather than empirical.
title: AI for software engineering: from probable to provable

rationale: Examines legal and policy issues for AI use in penetration testing and AI safety testing, transferable to security governance but outside AI-generated code oversight.
title: Legal, policy, and compliance issues in using AI for security: using taiwan's cybersecurity management act and penetration testing as examples
